coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/co-doped PANI binary nanocomposites as high-performance supercapacitor electrode materials

Çıplak, Zafer
Soysal, Furkan
Yıldız, Nuray
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a suitable and high-performance electrode material for practical applications was developed using a simple two-step approach. Here, we report that nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots with co-doped polyaniline (N-GQDs-PANI) were fabricated by in situ polymerization as supercapacitor electrodes and that the effect of the amount of N-GQDs in the electrode material on the electrochemical performance was investigated. The integration of N-GQDs into co-doped PANI structure prevented the agglomeration of PANI and enabled the fabrication of N-GQDs as thin film on the surface. This modification improved the charge transfer of the N-GQDs-PANI electrode compared to N-GQDs and pure PANI electrodes, allowing it to exhibit superior electrochemical performance. The N-GQDs-PANI electrode has a specific capacitance of 503 F/g at a current density of 5 A/g with a capacitance retention of about 91.9 % after 10000 charge/discharge cycles.

XPS spectra of survey scan a) N-GQDs, and b) N-GQDs-PANI6 (N-GQDs:aniline 1:6 w/w); c) C 1s peaks of N-GQDs, and d) N-GQDs-PANI6; e) N 1 s peaks of N-GQDs, and f) N-GQDs-PANI6.
TEM images and the particles size distribution (inset) of the N-GQDs.
a,b) FE-SEM images, and c,d) TEM images of N-GQDs-PANI6 (N-GQDs:aniline 1:6 w/w) nanocomposite.
BET analysis of adsorption-desorption isotherm and pore-size distribution curves (inset) of a) PANI, and b) N-GQDs-PANI6 (N-GQDs:aniline 1:6 w/w).

CV curves of a) N-GQDs, PANI, and b) N-GQDs-PANI (N-GQDs:aniline 1:1–16 w/w) electrodes at a scan rate of 20 mV/s; c) The specific capacitance of N-GQDs-PANI binary electros obtained from CV analysis; d) CV curves of N-GQDs-PANI6 electrode at various scan rates (10–400 mV/s).

The GCD behavior of a) N-GQDs, PANI, and b) N-GQDs-PANI (N-GQDs:aniline 1:1–16 w/w) electrodes at a current density of 5 A/g; c) The specific capacitance of N-GQDs-PANI binary electrodes obtained from GCD analysis.
a) Nyquist plots of pristine PANI and N-GQDs-PANI (N-GQDs:aniline 1:1–16 w/w); b) The equivalent circuit used to fit the Nyquist plots.

a) GCD curves of N-GQDs-PANI6 at different current densities from 5 A/g to 100 A/g; b) The specific capacitance as a function of current density for N-GQDs-PANI6; c) The specific capacitance as a function of the cycle number for N-GQDs at current density of 10 A/g, and GCD curves for 1st and 10000th cycle (inset); d) Ragone plot for N-GQDs-PANI6.
CV, GCD and EIS analysis results.
Energy and power densities of the cell for N-GQDs, PANI and N-GQDs-PANI at current density of 5 A/g.
The specific capacitance, rate capability, cycle performance, energy density, and power density values of quantum dots/ PANI binary nanocomposites in the literature.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/co-doped PANI binary nanocomposites as high-performance supercapacitor electrode materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a suitable and high-performance electrode material for practical applications was developed using a simple two-step approach. Here, we report that nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots with co-doped polyaniline (N-GQDs-PANI) were fabricated by in situ polymerization as supercapacitor electrodes and that the effect of the amount of N-GQDs in the electrode material on the electrochemical performance was investigated. The integration of N-GQDs into co-doped PANI structure prevented the agglomeration of PANI and enabled the fabrication of N-GQDs as thin film on the surface. This modification improved the charge transfer of the N-GQDs-PANI electrode compared to N-GQDs and pure PANI electrodes, allowing it to exhibit superior electrochemical performance. The N-GQDs-PANI electrode has a specific capacitance of 503 F/g at a current density of 5 A/g with a capacitance retention of about 91.9 % after 10000 charge/discharge cycles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The unique combination of high-power density, fast charging and discharging, long cycle life, operation over a wide temperature range, low maintenance, safe operation, and environmental friendliness make supercapacitors a more attractive energy storage option for various applications. Unlike conventional batteries, which store energy in chemical form, supercapacitors store energy in an electric field between two electrodes separated by an electrolyte in general. Most electrical double-layer capacitance (EDLC) supercapacitors can last for millions of charge–discharge cycles, which means they have a much longer cycle life than most batteries. Supercapacitors are also capable of operating over a wide temperature range, from −40 to 85 °C, making them suitable for use in extreme environments. They require minimal maintenance compared to batteries and can be charged and discharged repeatedly without suffering significant degradation. These make them ideal energy storage systems for applications that require long-term reliability, such as, renewable energy systems and electric vehicles [1–6].

Supercapacitors store energy using two different mechanisms: EDLC and pseudocapacitance (redox capacitance). The first mechanism is based on the EDL that forms at the interface between the electrode and the electrolyte. When a voltage is applied to the supercapacitor, ions from the electrolyte are moved to the surface of the electrode, forming a double layer of charge. The charge is stored in the form of an electric field and can be discharged quickly, providing a high-power density. The second mechanism is based on the pseudocapacitance that results from reversible redox reactions at the electrode-electrolyte interface. This involves the transfer of charge between the electrode and the electrolyte, resulting in the storage of charge in the form of Faradaic reactions. Pseudocapacitance can provide a higher energy density than EDLC but is typically slower to charge and discharge. In practical supercapacitors, both EDLC and pseudocapacitance mechanisms can be used together to overcome the disadvantages of both mechanisms and supercapacitors with superior performance can be obtained. The choice of electrode material and electrolyte play a critical role. While it is desired that the electrode material has a high electrochemically active surface area, high electrical conductivity, good mechanical strength, and excellent electrochemical performance, it is also essential to be cost-effective, chemically stable, and easy to prepare [5,7,8].

The materials used for the electrodes in supercapacitors with EDLC are typically porous materials, such as, activated carbon, graphene, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), etc., with a high surface area that can store charge in the form of an electric field. One of the most used materials for this purpose is graphene and its derivatives [7]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with quantum confinement and edge effects, which are tiny pieces of graphene smaller than 10 nm, are promising materials for supercapacitor applications due to their unique properties. The use of GQDs in supercapacitor applications is a relatively new area of research. The number of studies on GQDs in supercapacitor applications is increasing rapidly [5,9]. Studies in the literature report that the doping of nitrogen (N), boron (B), sulfur (S), etc. atoms to GQDs structures is an effective strategy to improve their electrochemical properties. The change in the performance of the electrode material in the corresponding electrolyte depends on several factors, such as, the type of doping atom (e.g., electron donor or acceptor), the amount of the doping atoms in the structure, and the specific doping configurations. For example, doping with nitrogen atoms can provide additional energy levels in the material, which can increase the electrical conductivity and change the optical properties. The doping of the heteroatoms to the structure improves the physicochemical properties and thus the overall performance of the supercapacitor. These properties are; i) incorporation of atoms into the carbon surface and lattice structure increases the number of active sites, wettability, and porosity, also significantly improves charge distribution, charge density, and electrical conductivity; ii) heteroatoms do not add significant mass to carbon structures but provide additional specific capacitance; iii) doped GQDs structures in the Nyquist plot show lower charge transfer resistance and smaller semicircular radius because of increased electrical conductivity and wettability; iv) doped GQDs structures are a cheaper and simpler alternative to other graphene-based structures [1,3,5,10].

Pseudocapacitance materials have redox active sites on their surface that can undergo reversible Faradaic reactions with the electrolyte, resulting in the storage of charge. The most used materials for pseudocapacitors are metal oxides (RuO2, MnO2, NiO, etc.) and conductive polymers (polypyrrole, polyaniline, poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene), polythiophene, etc.) [10–12]. Conductive polymers have attracted attention as promising materials for use in supercapacitors due to their high theoretical capacitance, fast charge/discharge rates, and easy production. However, to achieve the high electrical conductivity required for supercapacitors, conductive polymers must be doped or oxidized. The conductivity of a polymer is determined by the number of charge carriers (electrons or holes) available for conduction. In an undoped or neutral polymer, the charge carriers are localized and cannot move freely, resulting in poor electrical conductivity [11–14]. Acid doping is a kind of chemical doping process used to introduce charge carriers into a conductive polymer. In this method, a protonic acid is added to the polymer, resulting in the formation of a polaronic state that increases the electrical conductivity of the material. Common protonic acids used for acid doping include hydrochloric acid (HCl), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), and phosphoric acid (H3PO4). The doping process involves adding the acid to the polymer solution or film, which causes the acid to donate a proton to the polymer chain, resulting in the formation of a charged site. The doping level can be controlled by adjusting the acid concentration and doping time. Doping with acids is advantageous as it is a simple and inexpensive method that can be carried out under mild conditions. Additionally, the choice of acid for doping can also affect the stability, electrochemical behavior, and mechanical properties of the conductive polymer. Therefore, the choice of acid and doping conditions should be carefully optimized for each application [11,14–16].

PANI is one of the most studied conductive polymers due to its high electrical conductivity, good stability, and easy synthesis. The two main types of acids used to dope PANI are organic and inorganic acids. Organic acids, such as, camphorsulfonic acid (CSA), dodecylbenzenesulfonic acid (DBSA), and p-toluenesulfonic acid (pTSA), are commonly used for doping PANI [16–18]. These acids are preferred over inorganic acids because organic acid-doped PANI has higher solubility in organic solvents, which facilitates their use in solution-based processing methods. Organic acid-doped PANI exhibits high conductivity and good electrochemical stability. Moreover, a stable polymeric structure with better morphology can be prepared for practical applications. This makes PANI to be suitable for use in supercapacitor electrodes. Inorganic acids, such as, hydrochloric acid (HCl), and sulfuric acid (H2SO4) can also be used to doped PANI. This significantly increases conductivity and specific capacitance of PANI, but due to the corrosive nature of the inorganic acid dopants, it may have lower processability, lower dispersibility, and higher environmental impact than organic acid doped PANI. This causes inorganic acid doped PANI to have low cycling stability. Therefore, doping PANI with both organic and inorganic acids, i.e. co-doping, is an effective approach to fabricate electrode materials with superior properties, such as, good conductivity and processability [14,16,19].

The hybrid structure of GQD and PANI combines the advantages of both materials, resulting in a highly efficient and desirable electrode material for supercapacitor applications. The GQDs act as spacers between the PANI chains, preventing the aggregation of PANI and thus enhancing its electrochemical performance. Moreover, GQDs provide additional surface area and charge transfer channels for electrolyte interaction, resulting in increased capacitance. The hybrid structure also allows improved charge transfer and faster electron transport, leading to a higher power density. This is due to the synergistic effect of the two materials, as GQDs can facilitate the charge transfer between PANI and the electrolyte, while PANI provides redox capacitance and the electrical conductivity necessary for efficient charge transport and charge storage with pseudocapacitance mechanism. Overall, the hybrid structure of GQD and PANI is preferred as an electrode material in supercapacitor applications due to its high specific capacitance, high power density, and good stability [18,20–23]. Xing et al. obtained the N-GQDs structure by a bottom-up approach (pyrolysis) and then successfully prepared the N-GQDs/ PANI composite structure by in situ polymerization on the N-GQDs surface. They reported that the electrode PANI retained only 32 % of its capacitance after 2000 cycles during repeated charge–discharge cycles with a current density of 5 A/g, while the electrode N-GQDs2.5/PANI still possessed 80.2 % of its capacitance (439 F/g) after 5000 cycles [18]. Kuzhandaivel et al. prepared sulfur and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots/polyaniline (S,N-GQDP) nanocomposites as the supercapacitor material. They found that S,N-GQDP electrode in the three-electrode configuration had a capacitance of 645 F/g at a current density of 0.5 A/g, and the cycling stability was 90 % (1000 cycle) [22].

Herein, we report the fabrication of N-GQDs and co-doped PANI binary nanocomposites and investigation of their electrochemical performances for the first time in the literature. The combination of solubility and conductivity of N-GQDs with a wide surface area and a large number of active sites has many advantages over conventional carbon-based materials. On the other hand, the preparation of co-doped PANI with improved electrochemical performance by in situ polymerization of aniline monomer on the surface of N-GQDs prevented the agglomeration of the PANI structure. Therefore, this collaboration improved charge transfer between N-GQDs and co-doped PANI, which increased cycle stability and capacitance. Additionally, the effect of the amount of N-GQDs in the composite on the electrochemical performance was investigated. In this study, a viable and high-performance electrode material was developed for practical applications using a simple two-step approach. The synthesized N-GQDs-PANI6 binary nanocomposite showed improved capacitive performance compared to pristine N-GQDs and PANI. The binary nanocomposite provided a high specific capacitance of 503 F/g at a current density of 5 A/g with a capacitance retention of nearly 91.9 % after 10000 charge/discharge cycles.

Urea was purchased from Merck (Germany). Citric acid monohydrate (≥99.7 %) and ethanol absolute (≥ 99.9 %) were supplied from Isolab Chemicals (Turkey). The following materials were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany): aniline (99.5 %), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 96 %), dodecylbenzene sulfonic acid solution (DBSA, 70 wt %), ammonium persulfate (APS), poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF), and N-methylpyrrolidone (NMP). Spectra/Por® 6 Dialysis Membrane with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 1 kDa was used to remove impurities and unreacted raw materials of N-GQDs.

A simple one-step hydrothermal method using urea as the nitrogen source and citric acid (CA) as a carbon source was used to synthesize nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) [24,25]. Briefly, a homogeneous aqueous solution of urea and CA (urea:CA 1:1 w/w) was prepared. Then, the obtained solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon- lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 4 h. After the reaction, the reactor was cooled down to room temperature and the green mixture was filtered with a 0.22 µm microporous membrane. To remove impurities and unreacted raw materials, the supernatant was dialyzed in a dialysis bag with a MWCO of 1 kDa until the pH of the effluent was close to 7. The obtained N-GQDs solution was kept at 4 ºC for further use.

N-GQDs-PANI nanocomposites were fabricated using in situ polymerization of aniline monomer on the surface of N-GQDs, using organic and inorganic acids DBSA and H2SO4 together as co-dopants for the polymer matrix, for the first time in the literature. Briefly, DBSA (0.1 M) and H2SO4 (0.025 M) were added into the homogeneous N-GQDs aqueous solution under vigorous stirring at 4 °C. Then, various amounts of aniline monomer (N-GQDs:aniline 1:1–16 w/w) containing DBSA (0.1 M)-H2SO4 (0.025 M) aqueous solution was added dropwise on N-GQDs mixture. After that, APS aqueous solution (aniline:APS 1:1 n/n) was added dropwise to the N-GQDs-aniline mixture with continuous stirring. The mixture was kept at an average temperature of 4 °C and stirred overnight. After that, 30 mL ethanol was added to the resulting stable green dispersion with stirring and kept intact for 30 min. The precipitate obtained was washed several times with ethanol and deionized water by centrifugation, and then dried at 60 °C under vacuum [6]. The effect of N-GQDs:aniline mass ratio (1:1–16 w/w) on the structural and electrochemical properties of the binary N-GQDs-PANI nanocomposite was investigated.

The morphology of N-GQDs and binary N-GQDs-PANI nanocomposites were characterized using QUANTA 400 F field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) and FEI Tecnai G2 Spirit BioTwin high-contrast transmission electron microscopy (CTEM) operated at accelerating voltages of 30 kV, and 120 kV voltage, respectively. The chemical structure of the samples was investigated with Shimadzu 1601 ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) spectroscopy between 200 and 1100 nm, Shimadzu 8400-S Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) in the wavenumber range of 400–4000 cm−1 using KBr pellets, and Thermo Scientific K-Alpha X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) equipped with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source. To determine the crystal structures of the electrode materials, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on a Rigaku MiniFlex X-ray diffractometer with Cu-Ka irradiation (λ = 0.154 nm) in the range of 2 theta from 10° to 90°. The optical data of the N-GQDs were measured using an Agilent Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrometer with Xenon flash lamp (80 Hz) and Agilent Cary 60 UV-Vis Spectrophotometer, at room temperature. The specific surface area, pore volume, and pore size distribution of both PANI and N-GQDs-PANI samples were characterized using the Quantachrome Corporation Autosorb-6. The analysis relied on multipoint nitrogen adsorption data, applying Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method. The pore size distribution of the samples was assessed utilizing the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method. The determination of the total pore volume was accomplished through the examination of adsorption data at a relative pressure of 0.99.

The working electrodes were prepared as follows: 80 wt % of the sample, 15 wt % of carbon black, and 5 wt % of PVDF were mixed to obtain a homogenous slurry using NMP as the solvent, then coated by spray-casting onto stainless-steel disks having 1 cm2 surface area as current collectors. The working electrodes were dried for 24 h in a vacuum oven at 70 °C. The average amount of active substance on stainless steel current collectors was about 1 mg (Fig. S1).

The prepared working electrodes were electrochemically characterized using a symmetrical two-electrode configuration. Galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD), cyclic voltammetry (CV), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analyses were performed on a Gamry Reference 3000 potentiostat/galvanostat electrochemical workstation. The measurements were performed between −0.2 to 0.8 V in 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte, at various scan rates (10 – 400 mV s-1) for CV measurements. The GCD tests were carried out at various (5–100 A g−1) current densities. EIS tests were performed in the frequency range of 0.01 Hz - 100 kHz with an amplitude voltage of 10 mV, and Nyquist plots were obtained from the EIS data.

The UV–vis spectra of aqueous solutions of binary N-GQDs-PANI (N-GQDs:aniline 1:1–16 w/w), PANI, and N-GQDs are given in Fig. 1a. In the UV–vis spectrum of N-GQDs, there are characteristic peaks at 234 nm and 335 nm wavelengths resulting from the transition of the CC bond π→π * and the CO bond n→π * [26–28]. Shoulder-shaped peak between 340 and 360 nm correspond to π→π * electron transitions of PANI, the absorption band between 400 and 440 nm is associated with the protonation step of PANI chains, and the broad peak between 780 and 850 due to the presence of polarons formed because of doping process showing production of conductive emeraldine salt structure of PANI. The characteristic bands of PANI are clearly visible in the UV–vis spectrum of all binary N-GQDs-PANI samples. Additionally, it is seen that the characteristic peaks related to the π → π * transition of the CC are weakened and the n→π * transition of the CO bond of the N-GQDs structure could not be determined because of in situ polymerization of aniline of the surface N-GQDs [26,29–31]. The presence of all characteristic bands of PANI in the binary N-GQDs-PANI samples prepared with the in situ polymerization approach on the N-GQDs surface is the first indication of the successful preparation of the PANI polymeric structure.

The photoluminescence property of N-GQDs is one of the characteristic features of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). In Fig. S2, 340 nm excitation PL spectrum of N-GQDs aqueous solution and PL excitation spectrum (PLE) obtained with excitation at 440 nm are given. The PLE spectrum exhibited two bands at approximately 230 and 340 nm wavelengths, in agreement with the UV–visible spectrum of N-GQDs given in Fig. 1a. In the PL spectrum obtained by excitation at different wavelengths (Fig. S2b), it is seen that the PL of the structure is independent of the excitation wavelength. This behavior is caused by the regular diffusion regions in the structures, as well as the regularity of the particle size and the surface state of the sp2 clusters [25,32,33]. Additionally, the quantum yield of the N-GQDs sample prepared using Equation S5 in quinine sulfate reference was determined as 10.2 % (Table S1). The result of PL spectroscopy analysis revealed that N-GQDs having photoluminescence properties were successfully synthesized [25,26,32].

FT-IR spectra of N-GQDs, PANI, and N-GQDs-PANI16 are represented in Fig. 1b. In the spectrum of N-GQDs, 3417 cm−1, 3194 cm−1, 1660 cm−1, 1412 cm−1, 1350 cm−1, 1171 cm−1, and 1574 cm−1 peaks correspond to the -OH functional groups, N-H vibration, C-H stretching vibration, CO vibration in -COOH, C-N stretching vibration, C-O stretching vibration, and CC peaks, respectively. This is an indication of the presence of nitrogen groups on the surface of the GQDs structure and the successful nitrogen doping of the structure [24,34–39]. The FT-IR spectrum of the N-GQDs-PANI nanocomposite show characteristic peaks belonging to the pristine PANI structure and these characteristic bands were shifted to wavelengths compared to pristine PANI. Peak indicating quinone ring deformation of PANI shifted from 1573 cm−1 to 1570 cm−1, peak due to benzoid ring deformation shifted from 1496 cm−1 to 1489 cm−1, C-N tensile vibration shifted from 1303 cm−1 to 1296 cm−1, C-N+ stretching vibration shifted from 1242 cm−1 to 1249 cm−1, -NH+ stretching vibration shifted from 1125 cm−1 to 1111 cm−1, and C-H bond out-of-plane bending vibration shifted from 802 cm−1 to 794 cm−1 because of the good interaction between N-GQDs and PANI [40]. Additionally, it was determined that the broad peak at approximately 3400 cm−1, which is caused by the -OH group in the N-GQDs and PANI, is found in the spectrum of the binary nanocomposite. In addition to the peaks of the polymeric structure, it was determined that the peak caused by the N-H vibration at 3194 cm−1 of the N-GQDs structure shifted to 3225 cm−1 in the spectrum of the binary nanocomposite. Consistent with the UV–vis spectrum analysis, FT-IR spectrum supported the successful production of the binary N-GQDs-PANI nanocomposite.

The XRD diffraction pattern of N-GQDs, PANI and N-GQDs-PANI16 are presented in Fig. 1c-e, respectively. The N-GQDs pattern shows a wide peak at 2ϴ= 24.26°, attributed to the interlayer d-spacing of 0.366 nm according to Bragg's law and corresponds to the (002) plane of the graphite structure, which is consistent with the lattice spacing of the graphite [41]. The XRD diffraction pattern of the pristine PANI, which is synthesized in DBSA and H2SO4 medium, has three characteristic peaks centered at 15.08°, 19.93°, and 24.69°, which were attributed to the crystal planes (011), (020), and (200), respectively [42]. The XRD diffraction pattern of the binary N-GQDs- PANI structure exhibited similar crystalline band properties with PANI, indicating that the prepared N-GQDs surfaces are coated with co-doped PANI.

XPS was utilized to examine the compositional characteristics of N-GQDs and binary N-GQDs-PANI6. The survey spectra of N-GQDs and N-GQDs-PANI6 are presented in Fig. 2a and b, respectively. The C:O ratio of N-GQDs and N-GQDs-PANI6 are 2.34 and 5.07, respectively, and an increasing C/O ratio indicates that the amount of oxygen decreases during the polymerization of aniline. The presence of the S2p signal in the XPS spectrum of the nanocomposite (Fig. 2b) indicates that the doping acids DBSA and H2SO4 are doped into to the PANI during the in situ polymerization of the aniline monomer. In Fig. 2c, the C1s spectrum demonstrated four peaks assigned to C(O)-O, CO, C-N, and C-C/CC at 288.8, 286.5, 285.1, and 284.4 eV, respectively. In the high-resolution N1s XPS measurement of N-GQDs (Fig. 2 e), two bands at 400.3 eV and at 402.1 eV are observed, these peaks correspond to pyrrolic N and graphite N, respectively [25,27]. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectra of N-GQDs-PANI also showed four similar peaks with N-GQDs, as shown in Fig. 2d. This reflects the chemical bond state of carbon atoms in composite materials [18,43]. N1s XPS spectra of N-GQDs-PANI showed four peaks at 398.8, 399.4, 400.9, and 402.4 eV attributed to quinoid amine (–N = ), benzenoid amine (–NH–), and protonated imine groups (=NH+–, and –N+–), respectively (Fig. 2f). It is known that the conductivity of PANI is related to the presence of the protonated imine formed by doping acids of the aniline monomer. It was found that the PANI structure doped with DBSA and H2SO4 contains a protonated imine structure in the N1s spectrum of the N-GQDs-PANI composite. These observations indicate that PANI is in the protonated state in the binary composite. This situation is thought to increase the conductivity of the structure and create more active sites [18,44–46]. These results confirm the occurrence of chemical interactions between N-GQDs and PANI. In summary, the spectra of C1s and N1s together prove that N-GQDs were successfully combined in PANI.

The morphological characterization of N-GQDs was conducted using TEM and SEM analysis, as depicted in Fig. 3 and Fig. S3. The TEM images show the production of N-GQDs structures with a particle diameter of 2–5 nm (3.5 ± 0.66 nm average). Additionally, the SEM elemental mapping results (Fig. S3c–e) reveal the homogenous distribution of nitrogen (N), carbon (C), and oxygen (O) species on the surface of the N-GQDs. FE-SEM and TEM images of N-GQDs-PANI6 binary nanocomposite are given in Fig. 4. The SEM images show that the PANI was prepared successfully by in situ polymerization of the aniline monomer. By examining the TEM images, it can be observed that N-GQDs interact with the PANI matrix, which is like a thin film. As with the other characteristic analysis, TEM images demonstrate the successful production of ultrafine PANI matrix including N-GQDs having a uniform particle size distribution.

Fig. 5 presents the N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms of both PANI and N-GQDs-PANI6 samples. Notably, significant hysteresis loops and a type IV isotherm are observed in both samples, indicating the presence of both micro and mesopores [47,48]. The surface area of the PANI sample was determined to be 48.8 m2/g, while the introduction of N-GQDs nanoparticles into the polymer structure resulted in an increased surface area of 80.3 m2/g. The pore size distribution of the samples is depicted as an inset in Fig. 5. For the pure PANI sample, a bimodal pore size distribution is observed, with an average pore size of 16.4 Å, ranging from 10.2 to 21.3 Å and 23.9–54.5 Å. Similarly, the N-GQDs-PANI6 sample exhibits a similar pore size distribution, with an average pore size of 21.4 Å, ranging from 13.4 to 18.8 Å and 21.3–47.5 Å. The total pore volume of the pure PANI was measured as 0.16 cm3/g, whereas the N-GQDs-PANI6 sample exhibited an increased total pore volume of 0.23 cm3/g compared to the pure PANI structure. The presence of plenty of active centers in N-GQDs during the polymerization process contributed to the formation of composites with a larger surface area with higher pore volume. It is well-established that micropores enhance charge storage capacity, while mesopores facilitate the rapid penetration of electrolyte ions, enabling capacitive behaviors that surpass the ionic kinetic limits of electrochemical processes. The combination of high specific surface area, substantial pore volume, and porous structure of the electrode material can provide abundant active sites for electrochemical processes, short ion transfer paths, and low resistance charge transfer, ultimately enhancing the specific capacitance and speed performance of the electrodes [48–50]. Consequently, the N-GQDs-PANI electrode is anticipated to serve as a superior type of supercapacitor electrode material with enhanced energy storage capabilities.

Electrochemical characterization of the prepared N-GQDs, pristine PANI and N-GQDs-PANI binary nanocomposites was performed in a 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte applying two-electrode configuration. Fig. 6a and b show the CV curves of N-GQDs, pristine PANI, and N-GQDs-PANI electrodes prepared using different ratios of N-GQDs:aniline (1:1–16 w/w) in the potential window between −0.2 and 0.8 V and at a scan rate of 20 mV/s. The specific capacitance values of N-GQDs and PANI electrodes were calculated using Equation S1 and determined as 42 F/g and 382 F/g, respectively. The specific capacitance values of N-GQDs-PANI binary electrode are shown in Fig. 6c. These values are 276 F/g, 609 F/g, 750 F/g, 698 F/g and 454 F/g according to the increasing aniline:N-GQDs ratio. It was found that N-GQDs-PANI (1:4–16) electrodes have very high specific capacitance compared to PANI and N-GQDs-PANI1, which has the lowest aniline:N-GQDs ratio. Additionally, two pairs of characteristic peaks of these electrodes, corresponding to the leucoemeraldine/emeraldine transition in the ∼0.2 V region and the emeraldine/pernigraniline transition in the ∼0.5 V region, were more prominent compared to the other electrodes (Fig. 6a,b) [4,51]. Moreover, the CV curves of the N-GQDs-PANI6 electrode at scan rates from 10 to 400 mV/s are shown in Fig. 6d. Because of the excellent electrochemical stability and charge transfer properties, the redox transition pairs are still clear at high scan rates, and it was found that between 10 and 400 mV/s N-GQDs-PANI6 electrode exhibited superior rate capability (80.4 %).

Another method to determine the effect of the aniline:N-GQDs ratio on the electrochemical performance is GCD analysis. Fig. 7a and b show the GCD curves of N-GQDs, PANI and N-GQDs-PANI (N-GQDs:aniline 1:1–16 w/w) electrodes in the potential window between −0.2 and 0.8 V and at a current density of 5 A/g. The specific capacitance values of the electrodes were calculated using Equation S2, and these values for N-GQDs and pristine PANI are 10.7 F/g and 291 F/g, respectively. The specific capacitance values of N-GQDs-PANI1, N-GQDs-PANI4, N-GQDs-PANI6, N-GQDs-PANI10 and N-GQDs PANI16 electrodes are 185 F/g, 416 F/g, 503 F/g, 406 F/g and 387 F/g, respectively (Fig. 7 c). Both CV and GCD analysis revealed that the electrode material with the lowest aniline:N-GQDs ratio (N-GQDs-PANI1), had the lowest electrochemical performance. This may be because the N-GQDs-PANI1 electrode contains less PANI structure in the electrode material compared to pristine PANI and other binary N-GQDs-PANI electrodes. The insufficient amount of PANI, which has high capacitive properties in the electrode material, significantly decreased the value of specific capacitance. The increase in the aniline:N-GQDs ratio firstly increased the specific capacitance of the nanocomposite because effective polymerization of aniline on the surface of N-GQDs having an ultrafine polymeric matrix incorporated with conducting N-GQDs. This structure assists the formation of short ion transfer migration length in the electrode material and improve the participation of PANI in the electrochemical process. Further increase in the aniline:N-GQDs ratio cause formation of electrode materials consisting of thick polymer structure reflecting similar physical and electrochemical properties with pristine PANI. This structure causes decreased conductivity, longer ion transfer distances restricting charge transfer, and lower incorporation of the inner parts of the electrode material to the electrochemical process. In Fig. S4, we present the GCD behavior of the N-GQDs-PANI6 electrode at a current density of 5 A/g within two different operating potential windows: 0–0.8 V and −0.2 and 0.8 V. Despite similar specific capacitance values (532 F/g and 503 F/g), the energy density values of symmetric two-electrode cell differed significantly, with 11.8 Wh kg−1 and 17.5 Wh kg−1 (Equation S3), respectively. The expanded voltage window allows for a broader range of charge/discharge potentials, resulting in enhanced energy density and improved energy storage capabilities. This wider potential range facilitates access to a broader electrochemical capacity range and maximizes the utilization of active materials, contributing to higher energy storage performance.

EIS analysis is another important method for the electrochemical characterization of charge transfer properties of electrode materials in supercapacitor applications. The Nyquist plots of PANI and N-GQDs-PANI (N-GQDs:aniline 1:1–16 w/w) electrodes in 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte are given in Fig. 8a. The equivalent circuit model used to fit the Nyquist plot is shown in Fig. 8b. The intersection of the impedance spectrum and the real impedance axis in the high-frequency region corresponds to the equivalent series resistance (Rs). The ionic resistance of the electrolyte, the resistance of the electroactive material itself, and the contact resistance between the electrode material and the current collector constitute the Rs. The semicircles in the high-frequency region correspond to the charge transfer resistance (Rct) [52]. The results of the EIS analysis and the capacitance values of N-GQDs-PANI and PANI electrodes are summarized in Table 1. It was found that the N-GQDs-PANI6 electrode with the highest specific capacitance values has very low Rs and Rct values. The Nyquist plot of the N-GQDs-PANI6 electrode shows a steeper slope in the low frequency region compared to the other electrodes, indicating superior ion transfer capability. This is because the polymer covering the surface of N-GQDs surface has an ultrafine structure that assists to creating a short ion transfer migration length. This situation revealed that the N-GQDs-PANI6 electrode has a more ideal capacitor behavior than other electrodes. The incorporation of hydrophilic and highly conductive N-GQDs nanoparticles with pseudocapacitive properties into the PANI structure, which exhibits low capacitance, increased the conductivity of the electrode material, interaction of the composite electrode with the electrolyte ions, and the accessibility of the ions. The high rate of Faradaic reactions enabled the N-GQDs-PANI electrode to exhibit excellent charge transfer properties.

Cycle stability and rate capability are other fundamental parameters for determining the applicability of electrode materials for supercapacitors. The rate capability of the N-GQDs-PANI6 electrode with a mass ratio of 1:6 of N-GQDs:aniline, which exhibited the highest performance because of CV, GCD, and EIS analyzes, was investigated in the current density range of 5–100 A/g ( Fig. 9a). The N-GQDs-PANI6 electrode exhibited a capacitance of 488 F/g even at a current density value as high as 100 A/g and retained 97 % of its capacitance (Fig. 9b). Comparatively, the rate capability of the DBSA and H2SO4 doped PANI, N-GQDs-PANI1 (N-GQDs:aniline 1:1 m/m), and N-GQDs-PANI16 (N-GQDs:aniline 1:16 m/m) was evaluated within the current density range of 5–100 A/g. The obtained rate capability values were 41.2 %, 79.8 %, and 67.7 % for DBSA-H2SO4 doped PANI, N-GQDs-PANI1, and N-GQDs-PANI16, respectively (Fig. S5). Cycle stability of the binary N-GQDs-PANI6 electrode, which has high specific capacitance and excellent rate capability, was also investigated. Fig. 9c shows the change of the capacitance of the electrode with the number of charges–discharges cycles at a current density of 10 A/g. It was determined that the N-GQDs-PANI6 electrode retained 91.9 % of its capacitance at the end of 10000 cycles. The GCD curves at the 1st and at the 10000th cycles are shown in Fig. 9c as an inset. The N-GQDs-PANI6 electrode showed excellent electrochemical stability because there is not a significant difference in charge–discharge curves even after 10000 cycles related to the superior electrochemical performance and cyclic stability of N-GQDs-PANI nanocomposite. SEM image of the prepared electrode solution and the electrode material after the charge-discharge process are depicted in Fig. S6 . The electrode material retained its structural integrity. This aspect significantly contributes to its superior electrochemical stability, which is a noteworthy characteristic of the N-GQDs-PANI electrode. N-GQDs particles containing many edge defects and active sites also a crucial role in the polymerization of the aniline monomer, enhancing the interaction between the polymer structure and N-GQDs. It was suggested that the strong interaction between N-GQDs and PANI, and the fact that N-GQDs also improves the mechanical properties of the binary composite, play an essential role in preventing the swelling and shrinkage of the polymer structure PANI after the repeated charge–discharge process [22,33]. The Ragone plot showing the energy density and power density of the N-GQDs-PANI6 cell in two-electrode configuration is shown in Fig. 9d. At a current density of 5 A/g, the energy density of the cell is 17.5 Wh kg−1 (Equation S3), and the power density is reported to be 1881 W/kg (Equation S4) ( Table 2). The combination of N-GQDs with many edge defects and active sites with the PANI structure is an important approach to fabricate promising electrode materials for supercapacitor applications with high energy density. Here, it was found that the N-GQDs:aniline mass ratio is of crucial importance because the active sites of N-GQDs facilitate ion transfer and increase the interaction of the structure with the polymer in the production of composites. As shown in the TEM (Fig. 4) images, the ultrathin structure of the PANI polymer structure covering the N-GQDs surface further contributed to the rate capability of the electrodes by reducing the ion transfer distance. Because of the study, it was found that the N-GQDs-PANI6 electrode with a mass ratio of 1:6 of N-GQDs:aniline was the structure with the highest performance.

The electrochemical results of studies with reduced graphene oxide (rGO)-PANI, and graphene or carbon QDs-PANI for supercapacitor applications in the literature are listed in Table 3. The three-electrode configuration is an effective approach to study and compare the electrochemical properties of electrode materials. However, the calculated values for specific capacitance in a three-electrode conyuration are higher than the actual cell capacitance measured with a two-electrode system [53]. Since the two-electrode configuration better represents commercial capacitors in terms of both physical configuration and actual charge transfer mechanism, it is an effective method to determine the supercapacitor performance of electrode materials with higher accuracy. It is seen that the results of the studies in the table were obtained in the three-electrode configuration. In this study, for the first time in the literature, an N-GQDs-PANI binary nanocomposite was synthesized in DBSA-H2SO4 medium and its electrochemical performance was investigated in a two-electrode configuration. It was found that the fabricated N-GQDs-PANI6 electrode exhibited good specific capacitance and cycle life as well as superior rate capability compared to its equivalents. The co-doping process provides moderate conductivity for PANI chains and good processability to improve the interaction with the aqueous electrolyte at electrode-electrolyte interface. Moreover, the formation of N-GQDs structures with small particle size and homogeneous particle size distribution embedded in the polymer matrix, and the strong interaction between the composite components play a role in this excellent performance.

In this work, we have successfully synthesized N-GQDs using citric acid by applying a simple hydrothermal method and combined them with inorganic and organic acid-doped PANI by in situ polymerization to obtain high-performance supercapacitor electrodes. We observed that the composite exhibits an ultrathin film-like morphology on the N-GQDs surface, in contrast to the agglomerated PANI structure, thereby shortening the ionic transition length. The incorporation of N-GQDs into the electrode material with its solubility, large surface area, and multiple active sites not only improves charge transfer but also increases the specific surface area of the active material, which contributed significantly to the specific capacitance of the material. Moreover, it was found that the mass ratio of N-GQDs:aniline is a critical parameter for electrochemical performance. Because of the study, it was determined that the N-GQDs-PANI6 electrode with a mass ratio of 1:6 (N-GQDs:aniline) was the structure with the highest performance. With the synergistic effect of N-GQDs and co-doped PANI structures, the binary nanocomposite provided a high specific capacitance of 503 F/g at a current density of 5 A/g with a capacitance retention of about 91.9 % after 10000 charge/discharge cycles in the symmetric two-electrode configuration. Additionally, the CV analysis revealed that the N-GQDs- PANI electrode has a capacitance of 750 F/g at a scan rate of 20 mV/s and shows superior rate performance (80.4 %) between 10 and 400 mv/s rates. These results indicate that the N-GQDs-PANI dual electrode is a promising electrode material for supercapacitor applications.
